description: Multi-format content generation engine — competitor intelligence + copywriting brain powering ads, VSLs, event descriptions, and more

project_overview.what: CompeteIQ is a multi-format content generation engine. It combines a shared Quantum Scaling copywriting brain (business context, ICP, offers, voice) with format-specific brains (rules + examples per format) to generate high-quality content at scale. Formats include Meta ads, event descriptions, VSLs, and more. Competitor intelligence (Meta Ad Library scraping) is one input source — not the only one.
project_overview.why: Quantum Scaling produces 30-40 unique content variations per week across multiple formats. Manual creation is the bottleneck. CompeteIQ automates generation while preserving quality through a deeply seeded brain — compounding over time as format brains improve from feedback.
project_overview.end_state: A multi-format content studio where Lloyd selects a format, provides input (competitor URL, industry brief, or reference content), refines the output with quick feedback, and locks approved content. The system gets smarter as feedback accumulates per format. Eventually integrates with delivery channels and closes the performance feedback loop.
project_overview.current_summary: Frontend is built and deployed to Railway. Studio page generates calendar blocker content in bulk across 25+ audience segments with A/B/C variants. Library page organizes generations by run with collapsible groups, segment filter dropdown, VARIANTS label, per-item Save/Needs Work/Purge actions with toggle deselect. Next step: wire the generate button to the real backend endpoint (currently using mock data) and seed the universal + calendar brain in Supabase.

resume_here: Frontend live at Railway (competeiq-frontend service). Studio + Library fully built and UX-polished. Next: wire /generate/calendar-event endpoint to the frontend form — replace mock generation with real Claude call. Then seed universal brain + calendar format brain in Supabase so output quality is high.

current_phase.name: Phase 1 - MVP
current_phase.goal: Multi-format content studio: competitor ad browser + brief-driven generation. Format brain seeded. Single-turn refinement with brain promotion. Locked content library.
current_focus: Wire frontend generate button to real backend /generate/calendar-event endpoint
Seed universal brain (QS business context, ICP, offers, voice) in Supabase
Seed calendar blocker format brain (rules, examples, variant definitions)
immediate_next_steps: Connect StudioPage handleGenerate() to backend API instead of mock data
Build universal brain Supabase record with QS context
Build calendar format brain record with A/B/C variant definitions
Test end-to-end: input → API → Claude → Library

known_issues.title: Generate button uses mock data — not wired to backend yet
Existing backend schema built before UX design — may have structural gaps for multi-format architecture

next_phase.name: Phase 2 - Input Enrichment + Monitoring
next_phase.goal: Add URL-based input sources: blog post scraping, VSL transcript extraction. Scheduled competitor monitoring. Angle gap analysis.
key_features: Blog post URL → scrape body text as input
Video URL → extract transcript as input
Scheduled Apify pulls for competitor monitoring
Angle gap analysis — what competitors aren't saying
next_phase.prerequisites: Phase 1 custom app fully working. At least one format brain seeded and generating quality output.

deferred_items.reason: Complex scraping, ToS risk, not needed when text box handles manual paste
Must verify Descript has a public API first

future_phases.name: Phase 3 - Performance Feedback Loop (CreativeIQ Layer)
future_phases.goal: Close the generate → publish → measure → learn loop. Every piece of content gets a Content ID at generation. Performance data flows back in by channel. Format brains get reweighted toward proven angles. No ML required — SQL aggregations + Claude synthesis.

architecture.content_id_system: Every generated piece gets a UUID stamped at creation. This is the tracking anchor for all downstream attribution. Must be on every generated piece from Phase 1 onwards — schema decision should be made NOW to avoid migration pain later.
architecture.publish_event: User marks content as launched — records Content ID + channel + publish date. Low complexity. Just a UI action + DB record.
architecture.utm_generation: Auto-generate UTM string with Content ID at publish time: utm_content=[content_id]. Agency adopts convention. Required for paid attribution chain.

paid_tracking.source: Meta Ads Insights API (NOT Ad Library API — different product, requires ad account OAuth access)
paid_tracking.access: QS has its own Meta ad account run by agency. Agency grants API read access. Standard practice — same pattern as Triple Whale, Northbeam.
paid_tracking.metrics: CTR, CPL, spend — pulled by utm_content param to match back to Content ID
paid_tracking.meta_privacy_risk: LOW for internal tool. Meta's concern is training competing models, not business intelligence. Storing your own performance numbers is standard practice.

organic_tracking.fundamental_constraint: Most organic social platforms don't support clickable UTM links in posts. Attribution model for organic is fundamentally different from paid.
organic_tracking.instagram: No links in posts. Track engagement signals only (saves, shares, reach, impressions) via Instagram Graph API for Business accounts. Saves + shares are the meaningful intent signals, not likes.
organic_tracking.linkedin: Links in posts work, UTM params work. Closer to paid attribution.
organic_tracking.youtube: Links in description with UTM params work cleanly.
organic_tracking.implication: Paid feedback loop = conversion metrics (CPL, close rate). Organic feedback loop = engagement quality metrics (save rate, share rate). Different signals, both valuable, never conflate them.

architecture.angle_scoring: SQL: SELECT angle_type, AVG(ctr), AVG(save_rate) GROUP BY channel, angle_type. Claude interprets patterns. RAG retrieval reweights toward high-performing angles per channel. No ML required.
architecture.ghl_close_loop: Tag leads with Content ID at CRM entry via UTM. Query GHL API for show rate + close rate by Content ID. This completes the Revenue Causality Chain (content → lead quality → close rate). Requires UTM tracking discipline from agency.

open_questions_before_build: Agency Meta account: confirm API read access to Ads Insights (not just Ad Library) — RESOLVED: confirmed accessible
UTM convention: agency must adopt utm_content=[content_id] on every ad launch
GHL UTM capture: confirm GHL is capturing utm_content custom field at lead entry

platform_decisions.instagram: PHASE 3a — Controlled posting via Instagram Graph API. Business/Creator account required. OAuth once. No third-party stamp on posts. Engagement signals only (saves + shares are the meaningful metrics). No UTM links in posts.
platform_decisions.linkedin: PHASE 3a — Controlled posting via LinkedIn UGC Posts API. OAuth once. No stamp. Links + UTM params work in posts — closer to paid attribution. Company Page vs Personal Profile require different OAuth scopes.
platform_decisions.twitter_x: DEFERRED — API costs $100/month minimum for write access. Deprioritize until cost-to-value makes sense.
platform_decisions.facebook: DROPPED — Not a priority channel for QS audience. Facebook Pages API also stamps posts with 'via [App]'. Not worth the effort.

build_sequence: Phase 3a: 1. Add content_id to generation schema (Phase 1 decision — do NOW)
Phase 3a: 2. Publish event UI + DB table (paid = mark as launched, organic = schedule)
Phase 3a: 3. UTM generator at publish time (paid only)
Phase 3a: 4. Instagram OAuth + controlled posting via Graph API
Phase 3a: 5. LinkedIn OAuth + controlled posting via UGC Posts API
Phase 3a: 6. Engagement pull jobs (24h/72h/7d after each organic post)
Phase 3a: 7. Meta Ads Insights API pull (daily, matched to utm_content)
Phase 3a: 8. Basic performance dashboard (angle × channel × metric)
Phase 3a: 9. Angle scoring Layer 1 (SQL aggregations + Claude synthesis + human-approved brain updates)
Phase 3b: 10. GHL close-loop (lead tagging + show rate/close rate by Content ID)
tools_and_apis: Meta Ads Insights API (ad account OAuth — agency to grant access)
Instagram Graph API (Business account — OAuth, posting + engagement metrics, no stamp)
LinkedIn UGC Posts API (OAuth, posting + engagement, no stamp)
GHL API (lead outcomes by Content ID — Phase 3b)
Claude API (pattern synthesis — no ML required, SQL aggregations interpreted by Claude)
future_phases.prerequisites: Phase 2 complete. Enough published content for signals to be statistically meaningful (30+ campaigns minimum before patterns hold). Agency using utm_content=[content_id] convention consistently. Instagram Business account OAuth connected. LinkedIn company page OAuth connected.

future_phases.name: Phase 4 - Consumer SaaS
future_phases.goal: Multi-user onboarding, per-user brand brains. Turn CompeteIQ into a product.
future_phases.prerequisites: user_id already on all tables from Phase 1 — no schema migration needed.

recent_milestones.description: Library UX polish — VARIANTS label added before A/B/C tabs, checkboxes removed from segment rows (filter is top dropdown only), status toggle now deselects when clicking active state (Save/Needs Work is fully reversible)

Library page rebuilt as run-grouped table — 25+ segments stay organized with collapsible run groups, segment filter dropdown, A/B/C variant tabs under a single VARIANTS label, and Save/Needs Work toggle (click active status to reset)
Content Studio UI live on Railway — gradient dark design, Geist font, preset audience chips, bulk generation with runId stamping so all segments from one run group together in Library
Two CSS bugs diagnosed and fixed: Geist font self-referential CSS variable caused Times New Roman fallback; glow div absolute positioning without relative parent anchored to viewport
App interface fully designed and approved — includes content creation workspace, brain editor, and content library with format-specific workflows
Scope expanded from competitor ad browsing to a full multi-format content engine — now supports ads, event descriptions, VSLs, and future formats from a single tool
Production server deployment fixed and stable — app is live and accessible

decision_log.decision: Organic distribution platforms: Instagram (Phase 3a) + LinkedIn (Phase 3a). Twitter deferred. Facebook dropped.
decision_log.reasoning: Instagram Graph API and LinkedIn UGC Posts API both support controlled posting via OAuth with no third-party stamp — TOS compliant. Twitter costs $100/month API access, deprioritized. Facebook Pages stamps posts with 'via [App]' and is not a QS priority channel.

decision_log.decision: CompeteIQ controls organic posting (not manual + mark-as-published)
decision_log.reasoning: Controlled posting auto-stamps content_id at post time and auto-pulls engagement metrics back at 24h/72h/7d. Zero manual tracking steps vs. requiring user to come back and mark each post published. Cleaner data, less friction.

decision_log.decision: content_id must be added to generation schema in Phase 1 — not Phase 3
decision_log.reasoning: Every generated piece needs a UUID from day one as the tracking anchor for Phase 3 attribution. Retrofitting this later requires a schema migration on a table that may have thousands of records. Add it now while the table is empty.
decision_log.phase: Architecture — decided Phase 3 planning, applies to Phase 1

decision_log.decision: Organic and paid use fundamentally different feedback metrics — never conflate
decision_log.reasoning: Paid attribution chain: Content ID → UTM → CPL → GHL → close rate. Organic attribution: Content ID → Instagram/LinkedIn API → save rate + share rate. Trying to measure organic like paid (expecting conversion data) is architecturally wrong. Organic signals are engagement quality. Paid signals are conversion efficiency.

decision_log.decision: Library stores generations in localStorage (competeiq:library key) — not Supabase for Phase 1
decision_log.reasoning: Static Next.js export on Railway has no server-side session. localStorage is sufficient for single-user MVP. Migrate to Supabase when multi-user or cross-device sync is needed.

decision_log.decision: runId stamps all segments from one bulk generation so they group together in Library
decision_log.reasoning: 25+ segments × 3 variants = 75+ items. Without grouping by run, Library becomes unmanageable. runId = crypto.randomUUID() generated once per generate loop, stamped on all items.
Real-time generation progress, slide-in panels, context-aware chat state, multi-mode navigation, and brain update flows require React. Static HTML cannot handle this cleanly.

decision_log.decision: Three app modes: Studio (generate), Brain (edit brains), Library (browse locked content)
decision_log.reasoning: Brain editing needs its own space separate from content generation — business changes need to be applied to the brain without going through Claude Code. Library provides cross-format content history.

decision_log.decision: CompeteIQ is a multi-format content engine, not just a competitor ad browser
decision_log.reasoning: Competitor ad scraping is one input source for one format. The system must support event descriptions, VSLs, blog posts, and future formats without architectural rework. Format-as-primary-object unlocks this.

decision_log.decision: Format brain architecture: universal brain + format-specific brain, stored as text configs in Supabase
decision_log.reasoning: Separation of universal (QS context, ICP, voice) from format-specific (rules, examples) keeps each generation call small — only loads what's needed. No RAG required until a single format brain exceeds context window limits.

decision_log.decision: user_id on all tables from day one
decision_log.reasoning: Enables Phase 4 multi-user SaaS without schema migration.
